Notice Of Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From The First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Feb-25-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ZHAOHUA FENG's Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From The First Amended Complaint.

Defendant Zhaohua Feng's motion to strike those portions of the first amended complaint that seek punitive damages, including the "Punitive Damages Allegations" contained in paragraphs 15 and 16, is granted. (See Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63 [affirming order striking punitive damages allegations].) In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) The allegations in the first amended complaint that defendant drove a motor vehicle illegally using his cell phone and speeding through a busy street with heavy traffic, and that distracted driving is looked down upon and considered a dangerous and unsafe practice by many people, may amount to recklessness but do not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code 3294(c)(1). (See Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-10 [evidence that snowboarder who struck skier was not "despicable" so as to support punitive damages claim; even if snowboarder was reckless in traveling at high speed in rest area without looking in the direction he was heading, he did not hit skier intentionally and attempted to avoid her].) Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, relied upon by plaintiff, is distinguishable both because it was decided before the statute was amended in 1987 to require a showing of despicable conduct and on its facts. (Id. at 1212 & n.14.)

Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
